ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried shark fin as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed seafood ingredients is limited.

serving.description: 1 small serving (rehydrated, as used in soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried shark's fin used in shark's fin soup is approximately 20 g dry weight, which rehydrates to roughly 60–80 g and provides about 68 kcal, 16.8 g protein, and 36 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Foods — Shark's Fin, Dried (Food Code F03040032)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition database entry for dried shark's fin (cr_id F03040032). HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Carbohydrate is reported as -1 (not available) in the source; all other core macros are present. Saturated fat and sugar are not separately reported by HPB for this item and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for dried shark fin; these are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — not applicable for this food category
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dried shark's fin is a high-protein, negligible-carbohydrate food (carbohydrate not reported by HPB; composition is dehydrated cartilage and collagen with no starch or sugar). The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for dried shark fin, consistent with the food containing negligible fermentable carbohydrate. Per the Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by convention. No GI testing is applicable or necessary for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg per 100 g — below the 400 mg high-concern threshold for a dried ingredient used in small quantities
hypertension.guidance: At a typical 20 g dry serving, sodium contribution is approximately 36 mg, which is clinically negligible; however, patients should request low-sodium broth when consuming shark's fin soup, as the surrounding preparation typically contributes far more sodium than the fin itself.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content — dried shark's fin is essentially pure protein (83.9 g/100 g) with no measurable sugar or starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried shark's fin poses no glycaemic concern in isolation; patients should be aware that shark's fin soup preparations commonly include cornstarch thickeners that raise the glycaemic load of the dish, and should request minimal or no thickener.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content is high at 250 mg per 100 g, and the food provides no cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (shark fin is cartilage, not fatty tissue)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of dried shark's fin due to its high cholesterol content (250 mg/100 g), and should avoid high-sodium, starch-thickened soup preparations; the food provides no omega-3 benefit and should not be relied upon as a heart-healthy protein source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No anti-inflammatory factors present — no omega-3, no polyphenols, no fibre, negligible micronutrients; high cholesterol may be relevant for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried shark's fin is not recommended as a beneficial food for rheumatoid arthritis patients, as it provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients and its typical soup preparation includes pro-inflammatory starchy thickeners and high-sodium broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried shark's fin (鱼翅 / 魚翅, yú chì) is a dehydrated seafood ingredient derived from the cartilaginous fin of sharks, most commonly used in traditional Chinese shark's fin soup (鱼翅羹). The dried product has a translucent grey-to-white colour and a stiff, fibrous texture; upon rehydration it becomes gelatinous and silky with a mild, almost neutral flavour that absorbs the surrounding broth. Nutritionally, dried shark's fin is an exceptionally concentrated protein source at 83.9 g protein per 100 g dry weight, composed predominantly of collagen and structural proteins from cartilage rather than muscle tissue. Fat content is very low at 1.6 g/100 g, and carbohydrate is negligible, making it a near-pure protein food. Cholesterol is notably high at 250 mg/100 g. Sodium is 180 mg/100 g in the dried form, but the surrounding soup preparation typically contributes far more sodium. Calcium is present at 65 mg/100 g, reflecting the cartilaginous matrix. The food provides no omega-3 fatty acids, no dietary fibre, and negligible vitamins. Clinically, dried shark's fin is low-concern for glycaemia and hypertension in isolation, but moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease due to high cholesterol and absence of cardioprotective lipids. It is widely regarded as a luxury ingredient in Chinese banquet cuisine and is a culturally significant dish at weddings and formal occasions in Singapore, Hong Kong, and mainland China. Conservation concerns have led to declining use in Singapore and internationally.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in dried shark's fin?
Is shark's fin soup good for diabetics?
Is shark's fin high in cholesterol?
Is shark's fin soup high in sodium?
Can hypertension patients eat shark's fin soup?
What are the health risks of shark's fin?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database (cr_id F03040032), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The match is exact. Carbohydrate is reported as not available (-1) in the source; all other core macros are present. Saturated fat and sugar are not separately reported by HPB for this item; these are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules and documented in notes.
notes: carbohydrate_g is set to -1: the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database does not report a carbohydrate value for this item (input sentinel value -1 preserved). This is consistent with the food being predominantly collagen/cartilage protein with negligible carbohydrate; detailed carbohydrate breakdown is not available in HPB or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dried product as of 2026. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, sugar_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and sugar breakdown are not reported by HPB for this item and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for dried shark fin as of 2026. water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for dried shark fin not available in HPB or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (shark fin is cartilage with no plant fibre). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; gi_category set to not_applicable; University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI value exists for this food, consistent with negligible carbohydrate content. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are best-available defensible matches; SNOMED CT has no applicable code for this food product. Conservation note: shark fin harvesting is subject to international conservation regulations and is banned or restricted in several jurisdictions; this record is maintained for nutritional database completeness only.
